deals_cleared.merger_description: Adyen N.V. (**Adyen**) is a financial technology company headquartered in Amsterdam, The Netherlands. Adyen is active in the supply of a global payments platform that enables businesses to accept and process payments across online, mobile, and instore channels, and offers related products and services.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Adyen N.V. (Ayden) applied for a notification waiver in respect of its
proposed acquisition of 100% of the share capital in Talon.One
GmbH (Talon. One), as described in the transaction documents
provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Adyen is a financial technology company
headquartered in Amsterdam, The Netherlands. Adyen is active in
the supply of a global payments platform that enables businesses to
accept and process payments across online, mobile, and instore
channels, and offers related products and services.
The target, Talon.One is a software company headquartered in
Berlin, Germany. Talon.One is active in the supply of a promotion,
loyalty and incentive management platform through a 'software as a
service' (SaaS) model.
Following completion, Adyen and Talon.One intend to develop
functionality that would allow loyalty features to be integrated more
closely into the payment processing flow.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is no horizontal overlap between Adyen and Talon.One
in the supply of promotion, loyalty and/or incentive
management platform services
b. there is a low risk of foreclosure or other concerning vertical
or conglomerate effects resulting from exclusionary
conduct, bundling or tying of products and services post-
acquisition
c. there are alternative suppliers of payments platforms and
promotion, loyalty and incentive management platforms in
Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: The transaction concerns the proposed acquisition of sole control of Protego Holdco B.V. (**Protego**) by Ivera Bidco B.V., an entity indirectly owned by funds managed and/or advised by the Triton Group (**Triton**) (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Ivera Bidco B.V. (Ivera), an entity indirectly owned by funds managed
and/or advised by the Triton Group (Triton), applied for a notification
waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of 100% of the share
capital in Protego Holdco B.V. (Protego), as described in the
transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

Triton is an investment firm based in Luxembourg managing and
advising companies, primarily medium-sized businesses
headquartered in Central and Northern Europe, in particular in
Austria, Germany, Switzerland, the Benelux, and Nordic countries.
Triton focuses on businesses in three core sectors: Business
Services, Industrials and Health.
The target, Protego, and its subsidiary group, hold the Integris
Composites business (Integris). Integris is a global supplier of
composite armour systems and protection solutions for armed
forces and defence contractors.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is no horizontal overlap or vertical relationships
between Triton’s connected entities and Integris in the
supply of composite armour systems and protection
solutions for armed forces and defence contractors
b. there are several alternative suppliers of composite armour
systems and protection solutions to customers in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Zijin Gold (Group) Co., Ltd., (**Zijin**), a wholly-owned subsidiary of Zijin Mining Group Co., Ltd. (**Zijin Mining**), proposes to acquire an interest in Chifeng Jilong Gold Mining Co., Ltd. (**Chifeng**), so that post-acquisition Zijin Mining as a whole will hold approximately 25.85% of the total number of shares in Chifeng, and acquire controlling power in Chifeng.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Zijin Gold (Group) Co., Ltd., a wholly-owned subsidiary of Zijin Mining
Group Co., Ltd. (Zijin Mining), applied for a notification waiver in
respect of its proposed acquisition of an interest Chifeng Jilong Gold
Mining Co., Ltd. (Chifeng), as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).
Post-Acquisition, Zijin Mining will hold approximately 25.85% of the
total number of shares in Chifeng, and acquire controlling power in
Chifeng.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

Zijin Mining is a global mining company listed on the Hong Kong
Stock Exchange and the Shanghai Stock Exchange. It is primarily
engaged in exploration, mining and processing of mineral resources.
Its principal products include gold, copper, zinc, lithium, silver,
molybdenum and other metals such as iron ore concentrate, cobalt
and sulfuric acid.
Norton Gold Fields Pty Ltd (Norton), a wholly-owned subsidiary of
Zijin Gold International Company Limited (a company under control
of Zijin Mining), is a gold producer with mining and processing
operations in Western Australia. The Norton Group is one of
Australia's largest domestic gold producers with mining and
processing complexes located in Western Australia's Kalgoorlie gold
region.
Chifeng is a gold mining company listed on the Hong Kong Stock
Exchange and the Shanghai Stock Exchange. Chifeng owns 7
operating mines and a resources recycling company in China,
Southeast Asia and West Africa. Chifeng’s main product is gold, and
it also produces copper, lead, zinc, molybdenum and rare earths.
Chifeng does not have any operating mines, assets or subsidiaries in
Australia.
Zijin Mining and Chifeng both supply gold doré to customers in
Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. the parties’ combined share in the supply of gold doré to
customers in Australia is low
b. there are several alternative suppliers of gold doré to
customers in Australia
c. there is a low risk of foreclosure or other concerning vertical
or conglomerate effects resulting from exclusionary
conduct, bundling or tying of gold concentrate, gold doré and
gold bullion post-acquisition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

determination_table_content.details:

determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, BPEA IX, is a private investment fund controlled by EQT
Fund Management S.à r.l. (EQT), its alternative investment fund
manager.
The target, Kakaku.com, operates online price comparison and
consumer information platforms in Japan. Its indirect subsidiary,
Time Design International Pte Ltd, incorporated in Singapore,
generates commission revenues from Australian customers booking
bundled travel packages (such as airline tickets and hotel
accommodation) through its technology platform.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition

lessening of competition. In particular, there is no horizontal or
vertical overlap in Australia between the acquirer (or its connected
entities) and Kakaku.com (or its connected entities), including in the
supply of online travel booking services.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds

While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely

arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.

notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on

deals_cleared.merger_description: The Acquisition is a capital structuring transaction which will involve funds and/or vehicles advised and/or managed by affiliates of Blackstone Alternative Credit Advisors LP (**Blackstone Credit**) converting a portion of their existing debt interest in Medallia Parent, LP (together with its direct and indirect subsidiaries, **Medallia**) into a majority equity interest (the **Acquisition**) to recapitalise Medallia and support its continued operation. This is in light of Medallia’s pending financial inability to support its ongoing debt service obligations under the existing capital structure.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Blackstone Alternative Credit Advisors LP (Blackstone Credit)
applied for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed
conversion of its existing debt interest in Medallia Parent, LP
(Medallia) into a majority 56% equity interest, as described in the
transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Blackstone Credit, is part of the Credit & Insurance
segment within Blackstone Inc. (together with any of its affiliates or
affiliated investment funds, Blackstone). Blackstone is a US-based
investment management firm. It has interests in Prodege, a
consumer marketing and insights platform with a limited presence in
Australia, and Material+, which provides marketing strategy and
insight services including to customers in Australia.
The target, Medallia, is a US-based software company that
specialises in providing customer and employee experience
management solutions. Using its experience management platform
and AI-driven software, Medallia collects feedback about end-
customer and employee experiences through surveys, digital
behaviour analysis, and social media to provide actionable insights
to help its enterprise customers improve their employee and
customer experience.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver and had regard
to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is no horizontal overlap between the products and
services supplied by Blackstone Credit, Blackstone (or its
controlled portfolio companies) and Medallia in Australia,
and no material vertical relationships, and
b. while there may be a complementary relationship between
certain products and services supplied by Blackstone’s
connected entities and Medallia, there are alternative
suppliers of these products and services in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Hillhouse, through Hermes Bidco, proposes to acquire a majority interest in KMC Solutions Hong Kong Limited.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Woodward pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Hermes Bidco Limited, a subsidiary of Hillhouse Investment
Management, Ltd (Hillhouse), applied for a notification waiver in
respect of its proposed acquisition of a majority interest in KMC
Solutions Hong Kong Limited (KMC Solutions), as described in the
transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

Hillhouse is a global alternative investment manager that deploys
capital across multiple strategies, including public equities, private
equity, private credit, and real assets. Through its private equity arm,
Hillhouse invests in long-term global opportunities across the
business services, healthcare, consumer, and industrials sectors.
One of Hillhouse’s investments is in Ascentium, a Singapore-based
platform focusing on trust and corporate services in Asia-Pacific
region, including in Australia. It provides administration, compliance,
and business solution, which includes employer of record (EOR) or
Professional Employer Organisation (PEO) services.
KMC Solutions is a business that primarily operates in the
Philippines, supplying workspace and outsourced HR services
including to Australia. KMC Solutions supplies physical workspaces
in the Philippines, with strategic locations in financial hubs or
business districts. KMC Solutions manages these workspaces
through providing 24/7 access and facilitating reliable internet
network infrastructure. KMC Solutions also offers end-to-end
outsourced HR services centred around workforces in the
Philippines. KMC Solutions recruits individuals for companies
around the world (including Australian companies) interested in
establishing an offshore workforce primarily from the Philippines, as
well as Vietnam, Mexico and Colombia. It also handles related HR
services in the Philippines such as initial talent recruitment and
negotiation of employment contracts, onboarding, training and
continuous payroll services. As part of its end-to-end outsourced
solution, it offers EOR and PEO services.
EOR or PEO services allows organisations to establish and manage
an offshore workforce. An EOR service provider will be the legal
employer of the person being hired. In contrast, a PEO service
provider will enter into a co-employment relationship with the
organisation and the person being hired.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is a small horizontal overlap between Hillhouse and
KMC Solutions in the supply of offshore recruitment and HR
services to Australian customers
b. there are alternative suppliers of offshore recruitment and
HR services to Australian customers
c. there is a low risk of foreclosure or other concerning vertical
or conglomerate effects resulting from exclusionary
conduct, bundling or tying of offshore corporate services
and offshore recruitment and HR services to Australian
customers post-acquisition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Wipro Enterprises Private Limited (**Wipro**) proposes to acquire a 91% interest in Indeco Ind S.p.A. and its subsidiaries (**Indeco**). A sale and purchase agreement was executed on 9 April 2026. Pursuant to that agreement, Wipro has designated its indirect wholly owned subsidiary, Wipro Pari Italy S.r.l., as the direct acquiring entity.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Wipro Pari Italy S.r.l. (Wipro Pari), an indirectly wholly owned
subsidiary of Wipro Enterprises Private Limited (Wipro), applied for a
notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of a 91%
interest in Indeco Ind S.p.A (Indeco), as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Wipro, is a privately held company incorporated in India
that operates across a variety of sectors including fast-moving
consumer goods, lighting, hydraulics, industrial automation, 3D
printing, aerospace component manufacturing, and industrial water
treatment. Its hydraulics division designs and manufactures
hydraulic cylinders (i.e. hydraulic actuators that provide linear motion
when hydraulic energy is converted into mechanical movement) with
applications in construction and earthmoving, mining, lifting, forestry,
agriculture and trucking.
Wipro’s related entity, Wipro Limited, together with its subsidiaries,
provides consulting, engineering, IT, and business process services
in Australia.
The target, Indeco, is incorporated in Italy and is a manufacturer of
hydraulic demolition hammers and other excavator attachments.
Indeco operates in Australia through its wholly owned subsidiary,
Indeco Australia Pty Ltd, which sells hydraulic hammers and
excavator attachments (including compactors, grabs, shears,
pulverisers, mulching heads and multi-processors) as well as spare
parts. These products have applications in construction, mining,
demolition, recycling, land clearing, infrastructure and forestry.
Hydraulic cylinders are an upstream input into some excavator
attachments. However, Wipro does not supply hydraulic cylinders to
Indeco in Australia, or globally.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular there is no horizontal overlap or vertical
relationship between Wipro and Indeco (including their connected
entities) in the supply of any products or services in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

determination_table_content.details:
Allegro Funds Pty Ltd (Allegro), via Fantastic Group BidCo Pty Ltd,
applied for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed
acquisition of 100% of the shares of Fantastic Furniture Pty Limited,
FHL Distribution Centre Pty Ltd, Fantastic Manufacturing Pty Ltd, FF
Brands Pty Ltd, Fantastic Furniture – The Package Deal Kings
Limited and Fantastic Furniture Limited (together, Fantastic
Furniture), as described in the transaction documents provided as

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Allegro, is an Australian registered private equity fund
with $4.9 billion of assets under management in investments in
Australia and New Zealand. It specialises in investments in sectors
including food, education services, tourism, energy, healthcare,
professional services, business process outsourcing, and logistics. It
does not have current investments in the retail sector in Australia.
The target, Fantastic Furniture, is a furniture and bedding products
retailer in Australia. Its products are sourced from third-party
suppliers and manufactured locally at its own facilities, before being
distributed to its stores and directly to consumers throughout
Australia. It sells both ‘ready to buy’ and ‘made to order’ products.
Products are sold through Fantastic Furniture's online marketplace,
which offers instore pick-up or delivery, and through its 85 stores
located across metropolitan and regional areas in each Australian
state and territory.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular, there is no competitive
overlap between Allegro (including its controlled portfolio
companies) and Fantastic Furniture in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Goldcup 39483 AB (under name change to Prometheus BidCo AB), a
company controlled by IK Partners (IK Partners), applied for a
notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of all of the
shares in SELATEK Holding AB and its subsidiaries (Selatek), as
described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, IK Partners, is a European private equity firm which
invests primarily across Northern Continental Europe. Its portfolio
companies operate across a range of different business sectors. The
Wendel Group, a French investment firm, has a majority shareholding
in IK Partners.
The target, Selatek, is a Swedish company that operates across three
core business areas: Electrical, Automation and Security. Selatek’s
subsidiaries are active primarily as consultants, installers and
suppliers within the electrical engineering and building services
sector, as well as in the construction, servicing and installation of
automation and security solutions. Selatek’s only subsidiary with
sales to Australia is Deltatec AB (Deltatec). Deltatec is a Swedish
company which supplies limited programming services of control
systems involving water and sanitation engineering services in
Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is no or limited horizontal overlap and no vertical
relationship between IK Partners and Selatek (including their
connected entities) in Australia
b. Selatek has a small presence in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Alta Genetics Australia Pty Ltd (**Alta Genetics**) is an Australian subsidiary of the URUS Group. The URUS Group is a global company active in the agricultural sector, specifically providing products and services in bovine genetics and reproductive technology.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Alta Genetics Australia Pty Ltd (Alta Genetics), an Australian
subsidiary of the URUS Group which is indirectly controlled by funds
advised or managed by affiliates of CVC Capital Partners plc (CVC
Capital Partners), applied for a notification waiver in respect of its
proposed acquisition of 100% of the shares in AgriWebb Pty Ltd
(AgriWebb), as described in the transaction documents provided as
part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Alta Genetics, is an Australian subsidiary of the URUS
Group. The URUS Group is a global company active in the agricultural
sector, specifically providing products and services in bovine
genetics and reproductive technology. Another subsidiary of the
URUS Group, Valley Agricultural Software, Inc. (VAS), supplies
software and information solutions targeted to dairy herd
management for intensive dairy operations.
The URUS Group, and by extension its subsidiaries, are indirectly
controlled by funds advised or managed by affiliates of CVC Capital
Partners (together, the CVC Funds), a public limited company
incorporated in Jersey and listed in Amsterdam. The CVC Network is
a global alternative investment manager focused on private equity,
credit, infrastructure, and secondaries, and includes CVC Capital
Partners, each of its subsidiaries, and the CVC Funds.
The target, AgriWebb, is an Australian provider of livestock and
farming management software. AgriWebb’s software management
solutions are designed to assist farmers in managing their day-to-
day operational business with farm record keeping, farm mapping,
livestock management, pasture and grazing management, task and
workflow management, and farm auditing and compliance.
The parties are both active in the supply of livestock management
software in Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
certain publicly available information, and had regard to the factors
in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
(Cth) (the Act).
• Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is limited overlap between the product offerings of the
parties, as their respective offerings primarily serve different
use cases
b. there are alternative providers of the types of livestock
management software offered by the parties, both globally
and in Australia
c. VAS has a small Australian presence, and any increment in
share of supply arising from the Acquisition is estimated to
be small.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

Adelaide and South Australia more broadly
c. there is a low risk of foreclosure or other concerning vertical
effects resulting from exclusionary conduct post-acquisition.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Medtronic plc (Medtronic), via its indirect wholly-owned subsidiary Project Saint Merger Sub, Inc., proposes to acquire Scientia Vascular, Inc. (Scientia) (the Acquisition).

events.title: Medtronic - Scientia - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Medtronic plc (Medtronic), via its wholly owned subsidiary United
States Surgical Corporation, proposes to acquire Scientia Vascular,
Inc. (Scientia) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Medtronic, is a global healthcare technology company
incorporated in Ireland and listed on the New York Stock Exchange.
Globally (including in Australia), Medtronic develops, manufactures,
distributes and sells device-based medical therapies and services
within its cardiovascular, neuroscience and medical surgery
portfolios.
In Australia, Medtronic operates through its Australian subsidiary,
Medtronic Australasia Pty Ltd, to supply medical devices, including
neurovascular devices such as guidewires, catheters, aspiration
catheters, embolic coils, flow diverters, liquid embolic agents,
intrasaccular devices, AB stents and stent retrievers. In relation to
the supply of guidewires in Australia, Medtronic supplies its Mirage
.008” guidewire.
The target, Scientia, is a medical device company headquartered in
West Valley City, Utah, USA. Scientia specialises in microfabrication
technology for guidewires and catheters used in neurovascular and
peripheral vascular procedures.
In Australia, Scientia supplies guidewires under its “Aristotle” brand
through a third-party distributor, LifeHealthcare Pty Ltd. The
Aristotle-branded guidewires are supplied in Australia in four
diameter configurations, .014”, .018”, .024”, and .035”.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
Medtronic and Scientia both supply neurovascular guidewires in
Australia.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third parties and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• For the supply of neurovascular guidewires in Australia, the
market share aggregation resulting from the Acquisition is
estimated to be low, and post-Acquisition, the merged entity will
continue to face competition from multiple alternative suppliers
of neurovascular guidewires.
• Some market participants were concerned that the Acquisition
could give rise to conglomerate effects by enabling Medtronic to
bundle its existing neurovascular medical devices with Scientia’s
guidewires, resulting in foreclosure of rival guidewire suppliers.
• The ACCC has had regard to:
1. the position of the Parties in their respective markets,
including Medtronic’s position as a supplier of a portfolio of
neurovascular medical devices,
2. the presence of alternative suppliers of both neurovascular
guidewires and other neurovascular medical devices, and
3. customer procurement processes for neurovascular medical
devices.
• The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise
to conglomerate effects, as the merged entity would be unlikely
to have the ability and incentive to engage in various commercial
arrangements (such as offering bulk or multi-product discounts)
to the point of foreclosing rival neurovascular guidewire
suppliers and harming competition in the supply of
neurovascular guidewires.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Sydney Water Corporation (**SWC**) proposes to acquire from the Hawkesbury City Council (**HCC**) the following assets of the Windsor Sewerage Scheme in New South Wales:

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Sydney Water Corporation (SWC) applied for a notification waiver in
respect of its proposed acquisition of certain assets of Hawkesbury
City Council (HCC), as described in the transaction documents
provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).
The assets to be acquired are part of the Windsor Sewerage Scheme
and include the South Windsor wastewater treatment plant and the
pumping stations and reticulation systems required to supply
wastewater services to customers in the Hawkesbury City local
government area.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, SWC, is a state owned corporation established under
the Sydney Water Act 1994 (NSW) and State Owned Corporations Act
1989 (NSW). SWC provides water and wastewater services across
the greater Sydney metropolitan region, but it currently does not
provide wastewater services for most of the Hawkesbury City local
government area. SWC does provide drinking water services for
most of the Hawkesbury City local government area.
The HCC is a body politic of the State of NSW constituted under the
Local Government Act 1993 (NSW). HCC provides wastewater
services to properties in part of the Hawkesbury City local
government area through the Windsor Sewerage Scheme. The
Windsor Sewerage Scheme comprises two wastewater treatment
plants at McGraths Hill and South Windsor, 21 pumping stations and
the associated reticulation systems. The McGraths Hill wastewater
treatment plant is not subject to the Acquisition
SWC and the HCC do not compete for customers. Customers within
the Windsor Sewerage Scheme area of operations cannot currently
contract with SWC to provide wastewater services, and customers
outside the Windsor Sewerage Scheme area of operations cannot
contract with the HCC to provide wastewater services using the
Windsor Sewerage Scheme.
SWC and the HCC also acquire chemicals to be used in their
respective wastewater systems, in particular, aluminium
chlorohydrate, biosolids polymer ferric caustic soda, sodium
hypochlorite, and methanol.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is no horizontal overlap between SWC and HCC in the
supply of wastewater services to customers within the
Windsor Sewerage Scheme’s areas of operation in the
Hawkesbury City local government area
b. the pricing of wastewater services is regulated and set by
the Independent Regulatory and Pricing Tribunal of NSW
c. while the parties have a small overlap in the acquisition of
chemicals used in wastewater systems in Australia, any
increment arising from the Acquisition is very limited.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

determination_table_content.details:

Europa Holding, S.L. (together with its subsidiaries, Air Europa), as

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be

The acquirer, Turkish Airlines, operates in the aviation sector
globally. It provides passenger and cargo air transportation,
including international flights into and out of over 130 countries.
Turkish Airlines also conducts other related activities including
maintenance and repair operations, courier services, manufacturing
of aircraft interiors, and ground services. These services are
provided in Türkiye.
In Australia, Turkish Airlines provides international passenger air
transport services into, and out of, Melbourne and Sydney (either
directly or by way of cooperation agreements) and cargo air
transportation using the belly-hold capacity of certain passenger
flights.
The target, Air Europa, is a Spanish aviation group that primarily
operates international and domestic scheduled air services for the
carriage of passengers and cargo. Air Europa is a wholly-owned and
controlled subsidiary of Globalia Corporación Empresarial, S.A.
(Globalia). Globalia is a Spanish tourism group which does not have
any corporate presence or assets in Australia.
Air Europa does not operate any flights to or from Australia. Its
activities in Australia are largely confined to the sale of passenger
tickets for routes that are wholly operated outside of Australia (e.g.
Madrid–Paris), with some sales for international flights that include a
segment to/from Australia where that segment is operated by a
third-party airline pursuant to a codesharing/interlining agreement
for air passenger transport services.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian

the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).

the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is no direct horizontal overlap between Turkish Airlines
and Air Europa in the supply of international air passenger
transport services to and from Australia
b. Air Europa has a small, indirect presence in the supply of
international air passenger transport services to and from
Australia via codesharing/interlining agreements with a
third-party airline, however it does not operate the Australian
segment of any international flights
c. there are several alternative suppliers of international air
passenger transport services to and from Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

ongoing_phase_1.merger_description: 3M Company (**3M**) and funds managed and/or advised by Bain Capital Investors, LLC (**Bain Capital**) are proposing to form a new limited partnership (the **JV**), which will hold 3M’s supply of self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) and related products (the **SCBA Business**) and Madison Safety & Flow Holdings LLC (**Madison Fire & Rescue**). 3M will hold 50.1% and Bain Capital will indirectly hold 49.9% of the interests in the JV (together, the **Acquisition**).
